content.text:
Base directory for this skill: /Users/raunaksingh/.claude/skills/agent-reach

# Agent Reach — 互联网能力路由器

13 平台、多后端。**本 skill 存在时必须用它访问这些平台，不要自己发明方案。**

## 常驻规则（全程适用）

1. **动手前先体检**：多后端平台（小红书/Reddit/B站/Twitter）先跑
   `agent-reach doctor --json`，按各平台 `active_backend` 字段选命令组。
2. **声明你在用什么**：开始干活前说一句「使用 agent-reach 的 X 平台 / Y 后端」。
3. **失败按 references 里的重试链处理**，不要瞎猜命令。
4. **全网调研类任务**：组合多平台（Exa 搜索 + Twitter/Reddit 看讨论 + 小红书/B站看中文场景），并行收集再汇总。
5. **替用户盯版本**：完成一次较大的调研/多平台任务后，顺手跑
   `agent-reach check-update`（很快，一个 API 调用）。有新版就在收尾汇报里附一句：
   「Agent Reach 有新版 vX.Y.Z，复制这句话给我即可更新：帮我更新 Agent Reach：
   https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Panniantong/agent-reach/main/docs/update.md」。
   不要中断当前任务去更新，也不要重复提醒同一个版本。

## 路由表

| 用户意图 | 分类 | 详细文档 |
|---------|------|---------|
| 网页搜索/代码搜索 | search | [references/search.md](references/search.md) |
| 小红书/推特/B站/V2EX/Reddit | social | [references/social.md](references/social.md) |
| 招聘/职位/LinkedIn | career | [references/career.md](references/career.md) |
| GitHub/代码 | dev | [references/dev.md](references/dev.md) |
| 网页/文章/RSS | web | [references/web.md](references/web.md) |
| YouTube/B站/播客字幕 | video | [references/video.md](references/video.md) |

## 零配置快速命令

```bash
# Exa 网页搜索
mcporter call 'exa.web_search_exa(query: "query", numResults: 5)'

# 通用网页阅读
curl -s "https://r.jina.ai/URL"

# GitHub 搜索
gh search repos "query" --sort stars --limit 10

# YouTube 字幕（注意：B站不要用 yt-dlp，见 video.md）
yt-dlp --write-sub --skip-download -o "/tmp/%(id)s" "URL"

# V2EX 热门
curl -s "https://www.v2ex.com/api/topics/hot.json" -H "User-Agent: agent-reach/1.0"

# B站搜索（bili-cli，无需登录）
bili search "query" --type video -n 5
```

## 需登录态的平台（按 doctor 的 active_backend 选命令）

```bash
# Twitter 搜索（twitter-cli 首选；失败重试链见 social.md）
twitter search "query" -n 10

# Reddit（无零配置路径：OpenCLI 或 rdt-cli，必须登录态）
opencli reddit search "query" -f yaml   # 桌面
rdt search "query" --limit 10            # 存量/服务器

# 小红书（桌面首选 OpenCLI）
opencli xiaohongshu search "query" -f yaml
```

## 环境检查

```bash
# 检查可用 channel 与每个平台当前激活的后端
agent-reach doctor --json
```

## 工作区规则

**不要在 agent workspace 创建文件。** 使用 `/tmp/` 存放临时输出，`~/.agent-reach/` 存放持久数据。

## 详细文档

根据用户需求，阅读对应的详细文档：

- [搜索工具](references/search.md) — Exa AI 搜索
- [社交媒体](references/social.md) — 小红书, Twitter, B站, V2EX, Reddit（多后端命令组）
- [职场招聘](references/career.md) — LinkedIn
- [开发工具](references/dev.md) — GitHub CLI
- [网页阅读](references/web.md) — Jina Reader, RSS
- [视频播客](references/video.md) — YouTube, B站, 小宇宙

## 配置渠道

如果某个 channel 需要配置，获取安装指南：
https://raw.githubusercontent.com/Panniantong/agent-reach/main/docs/install.md

用户只需提供 cookies，其他配置由 agent 完成。

cd /private/tmp/claude-503/-Users-raunaksingh-Documents-realfast-insurance/3feceb2d-240d-4575-84e6-8e818cbd6451/tasks && python3 -c "
import json
d=json.load(open('wgf0k5irv.output'))
s=d['result']['synthesis']
print('=== VERIFICATIONS ===')
for v in s['verifications']:
    print(f\"[{v['layer']}] {v['check']}\n   -> {v['plain_english']} (scope: {v['in_our_scope']})\n\")
print('=== HOW TOP3 SUBMIT ===')
for h in s['how_top3_submit']:
    print(f\"{h['company']}: {h.get('channels','')} | form: {h.get('reimbursement_form','')}\")
print('\n=== REJECT VS DENY ===')
print(s['reject_vs_deny'])
print('\n=== IMPLICATIONS FOR C2 ===')
for i in s['implications_for_c2']:
    print('-',i)
print('\n=== KEY CITATIONS ===')
for c in s.get('key_citations',[]):
    print('-',c)
"

content.content:
=== VERIFICATIONS ===
[intake_reject] EDI/HIPAA syntax + structural integrity of the 837 (segments, delimiters, WEDI SNIP L1-2); reported via 999/TA1
   -> Is the submitted document even parseable and well-formed? A garbled file never reaches adjudication. (scope: no — we accept JSON over HTTP, not X12. The transport layer (JSON parse + schema validation) is our analog; a malformed body is a 400, not a persisted claim. The EDI machinery itself is out of scope.)

[intake_reject] Required fields present and correctly formatted (member ID present, dates well-formed, amounts valid, service code present)
   -> Are all the mandatory fields there and shaped right — member id, a service code per line, a valid date, a non-negative integer-cents amount? (scope: yes — this is the core of C2 intake. A request-body validator (e.g. zod) enforces it before any adjudication runs. Missing/malformed → reject.)

[intake_reject] Money/format validity — integer cents, non-negative, plausible date (not future-dated beyond tolerance)
   -> Is each billed amount a clean non-negative integer in cents, and is each service date a real, non-future date? (scope: yes — cheap, deterministic well-formedness checks that belong at intake. Reject on failure.)

[intake_reject] Member/subscriber match — member ID resolves to a known member record
   -> Does this member id correspond to a real member we have on file at all? Not whether their coverage is active — just whether the entity exists. (scope: yes — existence of the member is a well-formedness/identity question and belongs at intake-reject. (Whether the policy is ACTIVE on the service date is a separate adjudication question — see below.) This is fork #3.)

[intake_reject] Subscriber-name-to-policyholder consistency (e.g. 'Gene' vs 'Eugene')
   -> Does the name on the claim match the name on the policy record? (scope: no — we do not capture patient/subscriber name (PII minimized). Identity is established by member_id alone; there is no name to cross-check.)

[intake_reject] Duplicate claim/line detection at intake (same member/service/date/amount already submitted)
   -> Has an identical submission already been received? Real clearinghouses bounce exact duplicates before processing. (scope: maybe — this is fork #5. Can sit at intake (reject/flag a resubmission of the same fingerprint) OR surface during adjudication as a DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM line outcome. Our reason-code set already has DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM, which leans toward the adjudication-side, soft-flag model.)

[adjudication_deny] Member eligibility / policy active on the date of service
   -> Was the member's coverage actually in force on the day care happened? A known member whose policy had lapsed is denied, not rejected. (scope: yes — already step 1 of our adjudication order → POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE. This is the coverage-side counterpart to the intake member-existence check (fork #3).)

[adjudication_deny] Benefit coverage / exclusion — is the service covered under the plan
   -> Does the plan cover this service at all? Explicit exclusion vs no rule found. (scope: yes — adjudication steps 2-3 → EXCLUDED (explicit) or NO_COVERAGE (no rule / unlisted code).)

[adjudication_deny] Prior authorization / referral obtained where required
   -> If the service needed pre-approval, was it obtained? (scope: yes — adjudication step 4 → PRIOR_AUTH_REQUIRED when required and the per-line boolean is false.)

[adjudication_deny] Annual limit / visit cap remaining
   -> Has the member used up the plan's yearly cap for this service (e.g. 20 PT visits, $1,500 chiro)? (scope: yes — adjudication step 5 → LIMIT_EXCEEDED, unit-typed (visits whole, dollars straddle).)

[adjudication_deny] Cost-share / pricing — deductible, copay, coinsurance, OOP max
   -> Of the covered amount, how much does the plan pay vs the member, given deductible/copay/coinsurance and the out-of-pocket ceiling? (scope: yes — adjudication steps 6-9, the heart of the engine. Produces payable_amount_cents + DEDUCTIBLE_APPLIED/COPAY_APPLIED/COINSURANCE_APPLIED/OOP_MAX_REACHED.)

[adjudication_deny] Medical necessity / clinical appropriateness, NCCI bundling, Dx-to-procedure consistency
   -> Is the service clinically justified and consistent with the diagnosis, and not improperly bundled? (scope: no — requires diagnosis codes and clinical policy, both out of scope (no ICD capture). Coverage keys off service_code only. Documented divergence.)

[adjudication_deny] Timely filing — claim submitted within the plan's filing window
   -> Was the claim filed within the allowed days after service (e.g. 180/365 days)? A late claim is adjudicated then denied, not front-end rejected. (scope: no — not in our reason-code set or adjudication order for v1. Note as an easy future adjudication-side rule; deliberately omitted.)

[adjudication_deny] Coordination of benefits — correct primary vs secondary payer
   -> If another insurer is primary, apply their payment first. (scope: no — out of scope (assumption 2, single policy, no COB).)

=== HOW TOP3 SUBMIT ===
UnitedHealthcare (UHC): Three channels: (1) Online — myuhc.com member account → Claims & Accounts → Submit a claim (digital submit or download/print); (2) UnitedHealthcare mobile app; (3) Mail — printed form + itemized receipts to the claims address on the member ID card. Provider side bills electronically as X12 837P (EDI equivalent of CMS-1500). | form: UHC 'Direct Medical Reimbursement Form' (memberforms.uhc.com/DirectMedicalReimbursement.html); plus a CMS-1500-style 'Medical Claim Form' PDF.
Cigna: Member self-files only when the provider does not (out-of-network or foreign care). Channels: (1) myCigna online portal / mobile app (submit claim + attach documents); (2) Mail — the primary paper channel: completed form + itemized bills to the Cigna address on the ID card. In-network providers submit electronically (837P/837I via clearinghouse). Filing deadline 180 days domestic / 365 foreign. | form: 'Medical Claim Form' (form 591692c Rev. 11/2023); also a 'Direct Member Reimbursement (DMR) Claim Form' and a 'Member Medical Claim Submission Form'.
Aetna: Member self-files only for out-of-network (in-network providers bill Aetna directly). Four channels: (1) Online — Health.Aetna.com/digital-claims / Aetna Health app; (2) Mail — form + receipts to the claims address on the ID card; (3) Fax — 1-866-474-4040; (4) Phone — number on ID card for assistance. Must write member ID on every receipt; print in black ink, sign/date. Timely filing 365 days; reimbursement check up to 45 days. | form: 'Medical Benefits Request' medical reimbursement form (English & Spanish); separate Dental, Vision, and Prescription drug claim forms.

=== REJECT VS DENY ===
REJECT is a front-end / pre-adjudication refusal: the claim fails a technical or identity well-formedness edit (bad EDI syntax, missing or malformed required field, unparseable amount/date, unidentifiable member, or a detected duplicate) and is bounced BEFORE the payer's adjudication engine ever evaluates coverage. A rejected claim was never adjudicated, no benefit determination was made, it produces no EOB and carries NO appeal rights — the submitter simply corrects the technical error and resubmits. Mechanically it surfaces in acknowledgment transactions (TA1/999 for syntax, 277CA for claim-level pre-adjudication edits). DENY happens AFTER adjudication: the claim was clean enough to be accepted, processed, and evaluated against the member's benefits/policy, and the payer decided to pay nothing or only part — for a coverage, eligibility/active-policy, prior-auth, limit, medical-necessity, timely-filing, or cost-share reason. A denial is reported on the 835 ERA / EOB with CARC/RARC reason codes and DOES carry appeal (dispute) rights. The one-line rule (Stedi): once a claim passes validation and enters adjudication it can no longer be rejected, only denied. Conflating the two is the classic modeling mistake — a rejected claim is a malformed message about a claim; a denied claim is a well-formed claim with an adverse coverage decision. In OUR system: C2 intake = the reject layer (well-formedness + member existence → ACCEPTED/REJECTED); C3 adjudication = the deny layer (per-line coverage → APPROVED/PARTIALLY_APPROVED/DENIED). POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE is correctly a DENY, not a reject, because it is a coverage decision about a real member — only an unidentifiable member is a reject.

=== IMPLICATIONS FOR C2 ===
- FORK 1 — Rejection model (400 vs persisted REJECTED state): For a take-home v1, prefer a synchronous 400 at the API boundary for well-formedness failures (bad JSON, missing memberId, malformed cents/date, unknown member). The industry reject is literally 'never entered the system / no record adjudicated / no appeal' — that maps cleanly to 'never persisted, returns an error, not a claim'. Adding a persisted REJECTED state to the claim machine would contradict the very definition of a reject (it WOULD be in the system) and bloats the state machine the PRD froze (SUBMITTED→UNDER_REVIEW→{APPROVED|PARTIALLY_APPROVED|DENIED}→PAID). Recommendation: keep REJECTED out of the persisted claim state machine; reject = HTTP 4xx with a structured error body { errors: [{ field, code, message }] }. Document explicitly that a REJECTED claim state is intentionally absent because rejects are pre-persistence. If you ever need an audit trail of rejected attempts, log them separately, not as Claim rows.
- FORK 2 — Capture diagnosis_code / provider PHI or not: Do NOT capture ICD diagnosis codes or provider NPI/name in v1. Rationale traced to adjudication effect (PRD assumption 12, TRACK #9): v1 coverage keys solely off service_code; there is no medical-necessity, Dx-Px-consistency, NCCI, network, or fee-schedule rule that consumes a diagnosis or provider id, so capturing them adds sensitive PHI with zero math impact and contradicts the PII-minimization stance. Keep the line item to { serviceCode, billedCents, serviceDate, priorAuthPresent }. Call this out in self-review as the single biggest deliberate divergence from real claims, with the one-line upgrade path (add Dx + a medical-necessity rule together, never Dx alone).
- FORK 3 — Member/policy existence = intake-reject vs adjudication POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE: Split it cleanly along the industry line. Member ENTITY existence (does this member_id resolve to any member record?) is an identity well-formedness check → intake REJECT (400, 'unknown member'). Member coverage being ACTIVE on the service date is a coverage decision about a real member → adjudication DENY → POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE (already step 1 of the order). This matches the research: an unidentifiable member rejects at the front end; a known-but-ineligible member is denied. Implementation: intake validator does a cheap member-exists lookup and 400s if absent; the adjudicator does the date-bounded policy-active check and emits POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE. Keep these in two different layers — do not let 'unknown member' leak into POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE, and do not turn POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE into a reject.
- FORK 4 — Synchronous one-pass vs two-phase intake-then-adjudicate: For 4 endpoints, deterministic, <100ms, single-writer SQLite, a synchronous one-pass POST /claims is correct: validate (C2 intake) → if invalid 400 → else persist SUBMITTED, adjudicate each line (C3), aggregate, return claim + per-line results in one response. The real-world two-phase split (277CA acknowledgment, then later 835 remittance) exists because EDI is asynchronous and batched — that asynchrony is the only reason for two phases and it is explicitly out of scope. BUT keep the two layers as separate functions/modules even within the one HTTP call (a validateIntake() that can only reject, then adjudicate() that can only deny) so the conceptual C2/C3 boundary is visible in code and independently testable. One transaction, two logical stages.
- FORK 5 — Duplicate detection at intake vs adjudication: The research shows BOTH exist: an exact resubmission is a front-end REJECT (duplicate, never re-processed), while a true already-adjudicated duplicate surfaces as a duplicate DENIAL (CARC 18) at adjudication. Our reason-code set already contains DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM and TRACK Q5 leans 'soft flag'. Recommendation for v1: model duplicates at the ADJUDICATION layer as a per-line soft outcome (DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM, payable 0), keyed on a line fingerprint (member_id + service_code + service_date + billed_cents) — this preserves a visible, explainable record (consistent with the immutable-decision and EOB story) rather than a silent 400. Reserve true intake-level duplicate rejection (idempotency on a client-supplied claim key) as out-of-scope-but-noted, since v1 has no resubmission/idempotency-key concept. This keeps duplicate handling inside the explainable adjudication trail where disputes can act on it, and avoids putting business-logic duplicate detection in the well-formedness layer. Close Q5 as: soft flag at adjudication, DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM, fingerprint-based.

=== KEY CITATIONS ===
- https://www.stedi.com/blog/the-difference-between-claim-rejections-and-denials — canonical reject-vs-deny boundary ('a rejection stops a claim before it's adjudicated'; 'once a claim enters adjudication it can no longer be rejected, only denied')
- https://www.uhc.com/member-resources/how-to-submit-a-claim and https://memberforms.uhc.com/DirectMedicalReimbursement.html — UHC channels + Direct Medical Reimbursement Form
- https://www.cigna.com/static/www-cigna-com/docs/form-medical-claim.pdf — Cigna Medical Claim Form 591692c, required-fields and itemized-bill intake gate
- https://www.aetna.com/individuals-families/using-your-aetna-benefits/find-form.html and Aetna Medical Reimburse Form PDF — Aetna channels + Medical Benefits Request form

- https://x12.org/resources/requests-for-interpretation/rfi-2145-277ca-receiver-level-rejection — 277CA claim acknowledgment, the transaction that carries pre-adjudication rejects
- https://www.stedi.com/blog/what-are-claim-edits-and-repairs — clearinghouse front-end edits/scrubbing and duplicate handling

toolUseResult.stdout:
=== VERIFICATIONS ===
[intake_reject] EDI/HIPAA syntax + structural integrity of the 837 (segments, delimiters, WEDI SNIP L1-2); reported via 999/TA1
   -> Is the submitted document even parseable and well-formed? A garbled file never reaches adjudication. (scope: no — we accept JSON over HTTP, not X12. The transport layer (JSON parse + schema validation) is our analog; a malformed body is a 400, not a persisted claim. The EDI machinery itself is out of scope.)

[intake_reject] Required fields present and correctly formatted (member ID present, dates well-formed, amounts valid, service code present)
   -> Are all the mandatory fields there and shaped right — member id, a service code per line, a valid date, a non-negative integer-cents amount? (scope: yes — this is the core of C2 intake. A request-body validator (e.g. zod) enforces it before any adjudication runs. Missing/malformed → reject.)

[intake_reject] Money/format validity — integer cents, non-negative, plausible date (not future-dated beyond tolerance)
   -> Is each billed amount a clean non-negative integer in cents, and is each service date a real, non-future date? (scope: yes — cheap, deterministic well-formedness checks that belong at intake. Reject on failure.)

[intake_reject] Member/subscriber match — member ID resolves to a known member record
   -> Does this member id correspond to a real member we have on file at all? Not whether their coverage is active — just whether the entity exists. (scope: yes — existence of the member is a well-formedness/identity question and belongs at intake-reject. (Whether the policy is ACTIVE on the service date is a separate adjudication question — see below.) This is fork #3.)

[intake_reject] Subscriber-name-to-policyholder consistency (e.g. 'Gene' vs 'Eugene')
   -> Does the name on the claim match the name on the policy record? (scope: no — we do not capture patient/subscriber name (PII minimized). Identity is established by member_id alone; there is no name to cross-check.)

[intake_reject] Duplicate claim/line detection at intake (same member/service/date/amount already submitted)
   -> Has an identical submission already been received? Real clearinghouses bounce exact duplicates before processing. (scope: maybe — this is fork #5. Can sit at intake (reject/flag a resubmission of the same fingerprint) OR surface during adjudication as a DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM line outcome. Our reason-code set already has DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM, which leans toward the adjudication-side, soft-flag model.)

[adjudication_deny] Member eligibility / policy active on the date of service
   -> Was the member's coverage actually in force on the day care happened? A known member whose policy had lapsed is denied, not rejected. (scope: yes — already step 1 of our adjudication order → POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE. This is the coverage-side counterpart to the intake member-existence check (fork #3).)

[adjudication_deny] Benefit coverage / exclusion — is the service covered under the plan
   -> Does the plan cover this service at all? Explicit exclusion vs no rule found. (scope: yes — adjudication steps 2-3 → EXCLUDED (explicit) or NO_COVERAGE (no rule / unlisted code).)

[adjudication_deny] Prior authorization / referral obtained where required
   -> If the service needed pre-approval, was it obtained? (scope: yes — adjudication step 4 → PRIOR_AUTH_REQUIRED when required and the per-line boolean is false.)

[adjudication_deny] Annual limit / visit cap remaining
   -> Has the member used up the plan's yearly cap for this service (e.g. 20 PT visits, $1,500 chiro)? (scope: yes — adjudication step 5 → LIMIT_EXCEEDED, unit-typed (visits whole, dollars straddle).)

[adjudication_deny] Cost-share / pricing — deductible, copay, coinsurance, OOP max
   -> Of the covered amount, how much does the plan pay vs the member, given deductible/copay/coinsurance and the out-of-pocket ceiling? (scope: yes — adjudication steps 6-9, the heart of the engine. Produces payable_amount_cents + DEDUCTIBLE_APPLIED/COPAY_APPLIED/COINSURANCE_APPLIED/OOP_MAX_REACHED.)

[adjudication_deny] Medical necessity / clinical appropriateness, NCCI bundling, Dx-to-procedure consistency
   -> Is the service clinically justified and consistent with the diagnosis, and not improperly bundled? (scope: no — requires diagnosis codes and clinical policy, both out of scope (no ICD capture). Coverage keys off service_code only. Documented divergence.)

[adjudication_deny] Timely filing — claim submitted within the plan's filing window
   -> Was the claim filed within the allowed days after service (e.g. 180/365 days)? A late claim is adjudicated then denied, not front-end rejected. (scope: no — not in our reason-code set or adjudication order for v1. Note as an easy future adjudication-side rule; deliberately omitted.)

[adjudication_deny] Coordination of benefits — correct primary vs secondary payer
   -> If another insurer is primary, apply their payment first. (scope: no — out of scope (assumption 2, single policy, no COB).)

=== HOW TOP3 SUBMIT ===
UnitedHealthcare (UHC): Three channels: (1) Online — myuhc.com member account → Claims & Accounts → Submit a claim (digital submit or download/print); (2) UnitedHealthcare mobile app; (3) Mail — printed form + itemized receipts to the claims address on the member ID card. Provider side bills electronically as X12 837P (EDI equivalent of CMS-1500). | form: UHC 'Direct Medical Reimbursement Form' (memberforms.uhc.com/DirectMedicalReimbursement.html); plus a CMS-1500-style 'Medical Claim Form' PDF.
Cigna: Member self-files only when the provider does not (out-of-network or foreign care). Channels: (1) myCigna online portal / mobile app (submit claim + attach documents); (2) Mail — the primary paper channel: completed form + itemized bills to the Cigna address on the ID card. In-network providers submit electronically (837P/837I via clearinghouse). Filing deadline 180 days domestic / 365 foreign. | form: 'Medical Claim Form' (form 591692c Rev. 11/2023); also a 'Direct Member Reimbursement (DMR) Claim Form' and a 'Member Medical Claim Submission Form'.
Aetna: Member self-files only for out-of-network (in-network providers bill Aetna directly). Four channels: (1) Online — Health.Aetna.com/digital-claims / Aetna Health app; (2) Mail — form + receipts to the claims address on the ID card; (3) Fax — 1-866-474-4040; (4) Phone — number on ID card for assistance. Must write member ID on every receipt; print in black ink, sign/date. Timely filing 365 days; reimbursement check up to 45 days. | form: 'Medical Benefits Request' medical reimbursement form (English & Spanish); separate Dental, Vision, and Prescription drug claim forms.

=== REJECT VS DENY ===
REJECT is a front-end / pre-adjudication refusal: the claim fails a technical or identity well-formedness edit (bad EDI syntax, missing or malformed required field, unparseable amount/date, unidentifiable member, or a detected duplicate) and is bounced BEFORE the payer's adjudication engine ever evaluates coverage. A rejected claim was never adjudicated, no benefit determination was made, it produces no EOB and carries NO appeal rights — the submitter simply corrects the technical error and resubmits. Mechanically it surfaces in acknowledgment transactions (TA1/999 for syntax, 277CA for claim-level pre-adjudication edits). DENY happens AFTER adjudication: the claim was clean enough to be accepted, processed, and evaluated against the member's benefits/policy, and the payer decided to pay nothing or only part — for a coverage, eligibility/active-policy, prior-auth, limit, medical-necessity, timely-filing, or cost-share reason. A denial is reported on the 835 ERA / EOB with CARC/RARC reason codes and DOES carry appeal (dispute) rights. The one-line rule (Stedi): once a claim passes validation and enters adjudication it can no longer be rejected, only denied. Conflating the two is the classic modeling mistake — a rejected claim is a malformed message about a claim; a denied claim is a well-formed claim with an adverse coverage decision. In OUR system: C2 intake = the reject layer (well-formedness + member existence → ACCEPTED/REJECTED); C3 adjudication = the deny layer (per-line coverage → APPROVED/PARTIALLY_APPROVED/DENIED). POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE is correctly a DENY, not a reject, because it is a coverage decision about a real member — only an unidentifiable member is a reject.

=== IMPLICATIONS FOR C2 ===
- FORK 1 — Rejection model (400 vs persisted REJECTED state): For a take-home v1, prefer a synchronous 400 at the API boundary for well-formedness failures (bad JSON, missing memberId, malformed cents/date, unknown member). The industry reject is literally 'never entered the system / no record adjudicated / no appeal' — that maps cleanly to 'never persisted, returns an error, not a claim'. Adding a persisted REJECTED state to the claim machine would contradict the very definition of a reject (it WOULD be in the system) and bloats the state machine the PRD froze (SUBMITTED→UNDER_REVIEW→{APPROVED|PARTIALLY_APPROVED|DENIED}→PAID). Recommendation: keep REJECTED out of the persisted claim state machine; reject = HTTP 4xx with a structured error body { errors: [{ field, code, message }] }. Document explicitly that a REJECTED claim state is intentionally absent because rejects are pre-persistence. If you ever need an audit trail of rejected attempts, log them separately, not as Claim rows.
- FORK 2 — Capture diagnosis_code / provider PHI or not: Do NOT capture ICD diagnosis codes or provider NPI/name in v1. Rationale traced to adjudication effect (PRD assumption 12, TRACK #9): v1 coverage keys solely off service_code; there is no medical-necessity, Dx-Px-consistency, NCCI, network, or fee-schedule rule that consumes a diagnosis or provider id, so capturing them adds sensitive PHI with zero math impact and contradicts the PII-minimization stance. Keep the line item to { serviceCode, billedCents, serviceDate, priorAuthPresent }. Call this out in self-review as the single biggest deliberate divergence from real claims, with the one-line upgrade path (add Dx + a medical-necessity rule together, never Dx alone).
- FORK 3 — Member/policy existence = intake-reject vs adjudication POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE: Split it cleanly along the industry line. Member ENTITY existence (does this member_id resolve to any member record?) is an identity well-formedness check → intake REJECT (400, 'unknown member'). Member coverage being ACTIVE on the service date is a coverage decision about a real member → adjudication DENY → POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE (already step 1 of the order). This matches the research: an unidentifiable member rejects at the front end; a known-but-ineligible member is denied. Implementation: intake validator does a cheap member-exists lookup and 400s if absent; the adjudicator does the date-bounded policy-active check and emits POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE. Keep these in two different layers — do not let 'unknown member' leak into POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE, and do not turn POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE into a reject.
- FORK 4 — Synchronous one-pass vs two-phase intake-then-adjudicate: For 4 endpoints, deterministic, <100ms, single-writer SQLite, a synchronous one-pass POST /claims is correct: validate (C2 intake) → if invalid 400 → else persist SUBMITTED, adjudicate each line (C3), aggregate, return claim + per-line results in one response. The real-world two-phase split (277CA acknowledgment, then later 835 remittance) exists because EDI is asynchronous and batched — that asynchrony is the only reason for two phases and it is explicitly out of scope. BUT keep the two layers as separate functions/modules even within the one HTTP call (a validateIntake() that can only reject, then adjudicate() that can only deny) so the conceptual C2/C3 boundary is visible in code and independently testable. One transaction, two logical stages.
- FORK 5 — Duplicate detection at intake vs adjudication: The research shows BOTH exist: an exact resubmission is a front-end REJECT (duplicate, never re-processed), while a true already-adjudicated duplicate surfaces as a duplicate DENIAL (CARC 18) at adjudication. Our reason-code set already contains DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM and TRACK Q5 leans 'soft flag'. Recommendation for v1: model duplicates at the ADJUDICATION layer as a per-line soft outcome (DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM, payable 0), keyed on a line fingerprint (member_id + service_code + service_date + billed_cents) — this preserves a visible, explainable record (consistent with the immutable-decision and EOB story) rather than a silent 400. Reserve true intake-level duplicate rejection (idempotency on a client-supplied claim key) as out-of-scope-but-noted, since v1 has no resubmission/idempotency-key concept. This keeps duplicate handling inside the explainable adjudication trail where disputes can act on it, and avoids putting business-logic duplicate detection in the well-formedness layer. Close Q5 as: soft flag at adjudication, DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM, fingerprint-based.

=== KEY CITATIONS ===
- https://www.stedi.com/blog/the-difference-between-claim-rejections-and-denials — canonical reject-vs-deny boundary ('a rejection stops a claim before it's adjudicated'; 'once a claim enters adjudication it can no longer be rejected, only denied')
- https://www.uhc.com/member-resources/how-to-submit-a-claim and https://memberforms.uhc.com/DirectMedicalReimbursement.html — UHC channels + Direct Medical Reimbursement Form
- https://www.cigna.com/static/www-cigna-com/docs/form-medical-claim.pdf — Cigna Medical Claim Form 591692c, required-fields and itemized-bill intake gate
- https://www.aetna.com/individuals-families/using-your-aetna-benefits/find-form.html and Aetna Medical Reimburse Form PDF — Aetna channels + Medical Benefits Request form
- https://www.cms.gov/files/document/837p-cms-1500pdf and Noridian CMS-1500 ↔ 837 loop/segment crosswalk — canonical envelope vs line-item field structure
- https://www.rdpcrystal.com/sniplevels/ (WEDI SNIP levels 1-7) — the layered front-end validation edits that define the intake-reject layer
- https://x12.org/resources/requests-for-interpretation/rfi-2145-277ca-receiver-level-rejection — 277CA claim acknowledgment, the transaction that carries pre-adjudication rejects
- https://www.stedi.com/blog/what-are-claim-edits-and-repairs — clearinghouse front-end edits/scrubbing and duplicate handling

content.text: Research is back and it's decisive — it **validates your boundary** and, on one fork, **pushes back on your lean**. Let me write the findings to the artifact, then walk it discussion-style.

input.content:
# 02 — Domain research: claim intake structure & verifications

Synthesis of a web-research pass (2026-06-18) across **UnitedHealthcare, Cigna, Aetna**
member-claim pages + the **CMS-1500 / X12 837 standard** and clearinghouse edit literature
(Stedi, WEDI SNIP, Noridian crosswalk). Purpose: ground the C2 (intake) module in how real
payers take and verify claims. Raw multi-agent output in the session JSONL.

## The boundary the research confirms: REJECT ≠ DENY

> **Reject** = front-end / pre-adjudication refusal. The claim fails a technical or identity
> edit (bad syntax, missing/malformed field, unparseable amount/date, **unidentifiable
> member**, exact duplicate) and is bounced **before** adjudication. Never adjudicated, no
> EOB, **no appeal rights** — fix and resubmit.
> **Deny** = post-adjudication adverse decision. The claim was clean enough to process; the
> payer evaluated coverage and paid nothing/part (eligibility, prior-auth, limit, medical
> necessity, timely filing, cost-share). Reported on the EOB with reason codes, **carries
> appeal/dispute rights**.
> One-line rule (Stedi): *once a claim enters adjudication it can no longer be rejected,
> only denied.* Conflating them is the classic modeling mistake.

→ In our system: **C2 intake = the reject layer** (well-formedness + member existence);
**C3 adjudication = the deny layer** (per-line coverage). Note: `POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE` is a
**DENY**, not a reject — it's a coverage decision about a *real* member; only an
*unidentifiable* member is a reject.

## Canonical claim structure → what we keep

**Envelope (claim header)** — real payers carry member ID, patient name/DOB/relationship,
group/plan number, billing+rendering provider NPI/TaxID, COB/other-insurance, total charge,
frequency/resubmission code, prior-auth number, signatures/assignment-of-benefits.
**We keep only `member_id`.** Everything else is derived (policy), out of scope (COB,
assignment, provider/network), or PII we minimize (patient name). Total charge = *derived*
from line sum, never submitted (avoids a balancing-edit reject we don't need).

**Line item** — real payers carry CPT/HCPCS, DOS, charge, units, ICD diagnosis + pointer,
modifiers, place-of-service, rendering NPI.
**We keep `{ service_code, billed_cents, service_date, prior_auth_present }`.** Setting is
encoded in the service_code (EMERGENCY_ROOM, INPATIENT_HOSPITAL are distinct rules), so no
place-of-service field. **Diagnosis (ICD) is the single biggest deliberate omission** — v1
coverage keys off `service_code` alone; capturing Dx adds clinical PHI with zero math
effect. Upgrade path: add Dx *and* a medical-necessity rule together, never Dx alone.

## Verification taxonomy (sorted by layer)

**Intake — REJECT (well-formedness + identity; never adjudicated):**
- Required fields present & correctly typed (member_id, ≥1 line, service_code, valid date, amount) → **in scope**
- `billed_cents` non-negative integer; `service_date` real & not future → **in scope**
- `member_id` resolves to a known member (existence only) → **in scope** (fork #3)
- EDI/HIPAA 837 syntax (999/TA1), subscriber-name match → **out** (we take JSON, not X12; no name)

**Adjudication — DENY (coverage; carries dispute rights):**
- Policy active on DOS → `POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE` (step 1) · Covered/excluded → `EXCLUDED`/`NO_COVERAGE`
- Prior auth → `PRIOR_AUTH_REQUIRED` · Annual limit → `LIMIT_EXCEEDED` · Cost-share → payable split
- Medical necessity / NCCI / Dx-Px consistency → **out** (no ICD) · Timely filing → **out** (noted) · COB → **out**

## How the top-3 actually take member claims

All three are **reimbursement-model member self-filing** — the member files **only when the
provider doesn't** (out-of-network / foreign care); in-network providers bill electronically.

| Payer | Channels | Member reimbursement form |
|---|---|---|
| **UnitedHealthcare** | myuhc.com portal · mobile app · mail | "Direct Medical Reimbursement Form" + CMS-1500-style PDF |
| **Cigna** | myCigna portal/app · mail (primary) | "Medical Claim Form" 591692c + DMR form; 180-day filing |
| **Aetna** | Health.Aetna.com / app · mail · fax · phone | "Medical Benefits Request"; member ID on every receipt; 365-day filing |

The common gate everywhere: **member ID + itemized bill**. That maps exactly to our
`member_id` + line items.

## Recommendations on the 5 C2 forks

1. **Rejection model → `400`, never persist `REJECTED`** (research diverges from the earlier (c) lean). A reject by definition "never entered the system / no record / no appeal"; persisting a `REJECTED` Claim row contradicts that and bloats the frozen state machine. Both malformed shape *and* unknown-member → structured `4xx` (`{ errors: [{field, code, message}] }`). Want an audit trail of bad attempts? Log separately, not as Claim rows.
2. **PHI fields → do NOT capture diagnosis/provider in v1.** Line stays `{ service_code, billed_cents, service_date, prior_auth_present }`. Biggest deliberate divergence; flag in self-review.
3. **Member/policy split → existence = intake reject (`400` unknown member); active-on-DOS = adjudication `POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE`.** Two layers, don't let one leak into the other.
4. **Sync vs two-phase → one synchronous `POST /claims`**, but two separate functions inside it: `validateIntake()` (can only reject) → `adjudicate()` (can only deny). The real two-phase split exists only because EDI is async/batched — out of scope. One transaction, two visible logical stages.
5. **Duplicate → soft flag at adjudication** (`DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM`, payable 0), fingerprint = `member_id + service_code + service_date + billed_cents`. Keeps it in the explainable/disputeable trail. Closes Q5. (Intake-level idempotency key = out-of-scope-but-noted.)

## Key citations

- Stedi — reject vs denial boundary (canonical)
- UHC / Cigna / Aetna member-claim pages + reimbursement forms
- CMS 837P ↔ CMS-1500 crosswalk (Noridian); WEDI SNIP edit levels; X12 277CA

`member_id` + line items.

## Recommendations on the 5 C2 forks

1. **Rejection model → `400`, never persist `REJECTED`** (research diverges from the earlier (c) lean). A reject by definition "never entered the system / no record / no appeal"; persisting a `REJECTED` Claim row contradicts that and bloats the frozen state machine. Both malformed shape *and* unknown-member → structured `4xx` (`{ errors: [{field, code, message}] }`). Want an audit trail of bad attempts? Log separately, not as Claim rows.
2. **PHI fields → do NOT capture diagnosis/provider in v1.** Line stays `{ service_code, billed_cents, service_date, prior_auth_present }`. Biggest deliberate divergence; flag in self-review.
3. **Member/policy split → existence = intake reject (`400` unknown member); active-on-DOS = adjudication `POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE`.** Two layers, don't let one leak into the other.
4. **Sync vs two-phase → one synchronous `POST /claims`**, but two separate functions inside it: `validateIntake()` (can only reject) → `adjudicate()` (can only deny). The real two-phase split exists only because EDI is async/batched — out of scope. One transaction, two visible logical stages.
5. **Duplicate → soft flag at adjudication** (`DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM`, payable 0), fingerprint = `member_id + service_code + service_date + billed_cents`. Keeps it in the explainable/disputeable trail. Closes Q5. (Intake-level idempotency key = out-of-scope-but-noted.)

## Key citations

- Stedi — reject vs denial boundary (canonical)
- UHC / Cigna / Aetna member-claim pages + reimbursement forms
- CMS 837P ↔ CMS-1500 crosswalk (Noridian); WEDI SNIP edit levels; X12 277CA

content.text:
Findings saved to [claim-intake-research.md](ai-artifacts/02-domain-research/claim-intake-research.md). Now the discussion. 🎙️

## 🎯 The headline: your boundary is *exactly* how the industry models it

The research found a canonical, named split — and you nailed it:

> **Reject** = failed front-end edits, *never adjudicated*, no EOB, **no appeal rights**. → "a malformed message *about* a claim."
> **Deny** = adjudicated, decided no, **carries dispute rights**. → "a well-formed claim with an adverse decision."
> Stedi's one-liner: *"once a claim enters adjudication it can no longer be rejected, only denied."*

So **C2 = the reject layer, C3 = the deny layer.** This also resolves a subtle trap you'd flagged: **`POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE` is a DENY, not a reject** — it's a coverage call about a *real* member. Only an *unidentifiable* member rejects.

## 🏢 How the top-3 actually take member claims

All three are **reimbursement self-filing** (member files only when the provider doesn't — OON/foreign). The universal gate everywhere is **member ID + itemized bill** — which maps *precisely* to our `member_id` + line items. Validates the whole intake shape.

## 🔀 The 5 forks — recommendations (one diverges from your lean ⚠️)

| # | Fork | Recommendation | vs. your lean |
|---|---|---|---|
| **1** | Rejection model | **`400`, never persist `REJECTED`** — for *both* malformed shape and unknown member | ⚠️ **research overrides your (c)** → goes to (a) |
| 2 | PHI fields | **Don't capture** diagnosis/provider; line = `{service_code, billed_cents, service_date, prior_auth_present}` | ✅ matches |
| 3 | Member/policy | **existence → intake `400`** ; **active-on-date → `POLICY_NOT_ACTIVE`** (adjudication) | ✅ matches |
| 4 | Sync vs two-phase | **one synchronous `POST /claims`**, but two functions: `validateIntake()` → `adjudicate()` | ✅ matches |
| 5 | Duplicate | **soft flag at adjudication** (`DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM`), fingerprint-based → **closes Q5** | ✅ matches |

**The one to actually discuss — Fork #1.** Your instinct was a hybrid: `400` for malformed, but persist a `REJECTED` row for referential failures (audit trail). The research argues *against* persisting `REJECTED` at all:

- By definition a reject **"never entered the system"** — persisting a `REJECTED` Claim row *is the contradiction.*
- It would add a state the PRD **froze out** (`SUBMITTED → UNDER_REVIEW → …` has no `REJECTED`).
- Real payers carry the reject in an *acknowledgment* transaction (277CA), **not** as a claim record.
- If you want the audit trail → **log rejected attempts separately**, never as `Claim` rows.

**My recommendation: go (a) — pure `400` with a structured error body, no `REJECTED` state.** But your audit-trail concern is legitimate; if it matters to you, we add a lightweight `rejected_submissions` log table that is *not* part of the claim state machine. **Where do you land — pure `400`, or `400` + a separate reject log?**

## 🧱 The Claim phases I propose we confirm (the spine for planning)

```
1. INTAKE (C2)      receive POST /claims → validateIntake()
                    ├─ structural: fields/types, ≥1 line, integer cents, real non-future date
                    └─ identity: member_id resolves?   ── fail → 400 (no persist)
                    pass → persist Claim + LineItems as SUBMITTED
2. ADJUDICATE (C3)  per line: active → coverage → prior-auth → limit → cost-share → OOP → writeback
                    (claim transient UNDER_REVIEW)
3. AGGREGATE        roll line outcomes → APPROVED | PARTIALLY_APPROVED | DENIED
4. SETTLE           explicit settle action → PAID (reimbursement plan→member, gateway success assumed)
5. DISPUTE          member disputes a line → re-adjudicate, original preserved immutably
```

Phases **1–3** are one synchronous request; **4** and **5** are separate actions. This is the full claim lifecycle we'd break into a plan.

---

**Two confirms to move us to planning:**
1. **Fork #1** — pure `400`, or `400` + separate reject log? (the rest of the forks I'll lock as recommended unless you object)
2. **The 5 phases above** — does this spine match your mental model, or is a phase mis-cut?

On your confirm, I'll write the C2 decisions into **TRACK.md** (close Q5, log fork decisions) and fold the intake model + reject/deny boundary into **PRD.md** — then we're clear to plan. 🚦

content.text:
Let me clear the concepts first (staying on the brief), then the one doubt actually worth your call. Keeping intake-only as you asked. 🎯

## 🧩 Concepts — with the corrections you asked for

**Claim-processing system vs. adjudication → *different*.**
- **System** = the whole pipeline: intake → adjudication → aggregation → settlement → dispute.
- **Adjudication** = *one stage* inside it — the engine that decides, per line, *covered? how much?*

So adjudication is a **part** of the system, not the system.

**"Adjudication = the formal notice to the member" → small correction.** Adjudication is the **decision**. The **formal notice** is the **EOB (Explanation of Benefits)** — the member-facing statement of what was covered/paid/owed and *why*. In our PRD that's `GET /claims/:id/explanation`. So: adjudication *makes* the call; the EOB *communicates* it.

**Your ownership loop is right:** member submits → claim is persisted & owned by the insurer's system → checked against that member's policy + coverage rules → result (EOB) shown back to the member.

**"States aren't that simple" → correct.** Two reasons: (1) there are **two** machines — the **line item** (each independently APPROVED/PARTIALLY_APPROVED/DENIED) and the **claim** (status *derived* by aggregating its lines — that's why "3 covered / 1 denied / 1 review" → PARTIALLY_APPROVED); (2) **REJECTED isn't a claim state** (our Fork #1 lean — a reject never becomes a stored claim). The linear "submitted→…→paid" is just the happy path.

## 🔒 The one doubt to settle now — diagnosis code

The tension you sensed:
- **Brief says** claims contain *"member names, diagnosis codes, provider details — sensitive health data."*
- **Research said** omit diagnosis (changes no adjudication math in v1).

They needn't conflict: capturing **`diagnosis_code` as a sensitive, encrypted, captured-but-not-adjudicated field** is the *cleanest way to demonstrate* the PHI handling the brief is actually testing — real PHI to encrypt & minimize, even though no rule reads it.

**My lean: capture `diagnosis_code` + member name as encrypted PHI, never used in adjudication.** Omitting is "cleaner" but skips the exact thing the brief flags. **Where do you land?**

## ✅ Intake scope (locking now, adjudication deferred)

`accept claim + line items` → `structural validation` → `member/policy resolution (existence → 400 if unknown)` → `compute duplicate fingerprint`.

Sub-note: computing the fingerprint **at intake** is fine; *acting* on a dup (hard reject at intake vs. soft `DUPLICATE_LINE_ITEM` at adjudication) is the open bit — research leaned soft-flag for explainability. **Park the *decision* with adjudication, just compute the fingerprint now?**

---

**Two answers and intake is solid:** **(1)** diagnosis code — capture-encrypted or omit? **(2)** fingerprint at intake but decide-on-dup at adjudication — OK? Nothing written to PRD/TRACK until you call these. 🚦

  <!-- Claim → LineItem -->
  <line x1="280" y1="596" x2="320" y2="596" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1.5" marker-end="url(#arrow2)"/>
  <text x="300" y="588" font-size="10" fill="#64748b" text-anchor="middle">1:N</text>

  <!-- Dispute → Adjudication (re-adjudicates) -->
  <line x1="690" y1="460" x2="690" y2="490" stroke="#94a3b8" stroke-width="1.5" stroke-dasharray="4 3"/>
  <text x="700" y="478" font-size="10" fill="#64748b" font-style="italic">reopens</text>

  <!-- footer -->
  <text x="40" y="690" font-size="12" fill="#64748b" font-style="italic">Adjudication context is pure. Submission context is transactional. The seam is the only place I/O happens.</text>
</svg>

89	    <text x="60" y="108" font-size="13" font-family="monospace" fill="#92400e">- $26.00  your 20% coinsurance on the remainder</text>
90	    <text x="60" y="130" font-size="13" font-family="monospace" font-weight="700" fill="#0f766e">= $104.00 paid by your insurer · APPROVED</text>

92
